nativeRange.habitat: Bleher's Rainbowfish inhabits the shallow, plant-rich margins of lowland lakes in the lower Mamberamo River system of West Papua. The water is clear to slightly turbid and warm, with abundant aquatic and marginal vegetation, in a foothill region surrounded by dense jungle.

decorPreferences: dense marginal planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, Echinodorus)
open central swimming area at least 60 cm wide
dark substrate to intensify male coloration
floating plants or surface cover to diffuse lighting
driftwood or root tangles as retreat structure

diet.notes: Bleher's Rainbowfish accepts a wide variety of prepared and live or frozen foods. A staple of high-quality small pellets or flake food with a spirulina component works well as a base diet, supplemented with frozen or live daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, and micro-pellets. In the wild, the species forages on zooplankton, insects, algae, and organic detritus. Feeding small amounts two to three times daily, with variety throughout the week, tends to support the most intense male coloration.

avoidWith: very small fish or fry (may be predated)

summary: Bleher’s Rainbowfish (Chilatherina bleheri) is a narrow endemic from Lake Holmes in West Papua, Indonesia. Males develop vivid red-orange and blue-green iridescent coloration, especially during courtship. The species is listed as Endangered by the IUCN due to its extremely restricted range; captive-bred stock is increasingly available.
careNotes:
Bleher’s Rainbowfish (Chilatherina bleheri) was described by G. R. Allen in 1985 and named in honour of ichthyologist and explorer Heiko Bleher. It belongs to the genus Chilatherina, which is distinct from the larger rainbowfish genus Melanotaenia; both are members of the family Melanotaeniidae. The species is endemic to Lake Holmes (Danau Bira) in the lower Mamberamo River drainage of West Papua, Indonesia, with some reports extending to nearby Lake Tage. This extremely restricted range is the primary reason the species was assessed as Endangered by the IUCN in 2019.

In the aquarium, a group of at least six — ideally with more females than males, such as a 2:4 or 3:6 ratio — should be kept in a tank no shorter than 120 cm to accommodate the vigorous open-water swimming characteristic of rainbowfishes. Males develop red-orange flanks with blue-green iridescence on the dorsal surface when in display condition; a dark substrate and dense background planting tend to intensify this coloration. Water should be maintained on the slightly alkaline, moderately hard side — pH 7.0–8.0, gH 8–15, temperature 23–28°C — to reflect conditions in the Mamberamo lowland lake system. Bleher’s Rainbowfish is generally considered a peaceful mid-water shoaler and combines well with other similarly sized rainbowfishes, tetras, and calm catfish species. Given its Endangered conservation status, sourcing captive-bred specimens is strongly encouraged; they are increasingly available through specialist breeders and rainbowfish societies.
breedingNotes: Bleher’s Rainbowfish is an egg-scatterer that broadcasts adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over several days. Conditioning adults on live and frozen foods (daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms) stimulates spawning behaviour. Eggs typically hatch in 7–10 days at 25–27°C. Fry are small and require infusoria or commercially prepared fry foods for the first 1–2 weeks before graduating to baby brine shrimp and micro-pellets. Removing eggs or spawning mops daily to a separate raising tank significantly improves fry survival, as adults will consume eggs given the opportunity. Captive breeding is considered moderately achievable and is actively practised by rainbowfish enthusiasts to reduce pressure on wild populations.

primary.name: FishBase — Chilatherina bleheri (species ID 25601)
primary.notes: Primary data source. Provided maximum size (12 cm SL), temperature range (23–27°C), pH (7.0), hardness (dH 8–15), native range (Lake Holmes, lower Mamberamo, Irian Jaya/Indonesia), taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae, Atheriniformes), IUCN Endangered status (assessed 2019, criteria B1ab(v)), trophic level 2.8 (omnivore), and medium population resilience. Temperature upper end extended to 28°C based on general Melanotaeniidae thermal tolerance consistent with Aquarium Co-Op rainbowfish care guide (24–28°C). pH upper bound set to 8.0 based on ecological context of Mamberamo lowland lake system.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rainbowfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Melanotaeniidae care guide. Provided tank size recommendation (minimum 4-ft/120 cm), temperature 24–28°C (75–82°F), pH 6–8, hardness 8–20 dGH, schooling requirements (groups of 6+, more females), diet preferences (variety of dry/frozen/live foods), and compatible tankmates. No Chilatherina bleheri-specific page exists; data used to contextualise and extend FishBase parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chilatherina bleheri
additional.notes: Confirmed species etymology (named for Heiko Bleher), habitat description (shallow water with abundant plant life in foothill jungle region), and that three new rainbowfish species were described by Allen in 1985 from this area. Confirmed Lake Holmes distribution.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Chilatherina bleheri
additional.notes: Confirmed Endangered (EN) status with assessment date December 5, 2019, criteria B1ab(v). Species not evaluated under CITES or CMS. Restricted endemic range is the primary threat driver.

additional.name: FishBase — Chilatherina bleheri Photos (species ID 25601)
additional.notes: Two photos available by photographer E. Prins (Belgium). Image Chble_u9.jpg shows specimen at 8 cm TL taken November 2000. All rights reserved; permission required for reproduction.

fish.breedingNotes: Bleher's Rainbowfish scatters adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Conditioning adults on live and frozen foods beforehand stimulates spawning. Removing eggs or mops to a separate raising tank each day improves fry survival, as adults will consume eggs. Newly hatched fry require infusoria or prepared fry foods before moving on to baby brine shrimp. Captive breeding is increasingly practised among rainbowfish enthusiasts as a way to support conservation of this Endangered species.
